royal-birkdale.title: Royal Birkdale Golf Club — Visitor Guide, Green Fees & Tee Times
royal-birkdale.desc: Host of The Open Championship 2026. Visitor green fee £300–£350. Par 70, 7,156 yards. Full visitor policy, how to book and post-round guide.

hillside.title: Hillside Golf Club Southport — Visitor Guide & Green Fees
hillside.desc: The finest links course you've never heard of. Green fee £100–£150. Par 72, 6,850 yards. Full visitor policy and course guide.

formby.title: Formby Golf Club — Visitor Guide, Green Fees & Tee Times
formby.desc: A private gem — one of England's finest inland links. Green fee £160–£210. Par 72, 6,893 yards. Visitor policy and booking guide.

west-lancashire.title: West Lancashire Golf Club — Visitor Green Fees & Tee Times
west-lancashire.desc: One of England's oldest clubs — links golf at its purest. Green fee £80–£130. Par 72, 6,756 yards. Full visitor guide.

southport-ainsdale.title: Southport & Ainsdale Golf Club — Visitor Guide & Green Fees
southport-ainsdale.desc: Ryder Cup heritage and genuine links character. Green fee £65–£100. Par 72, 6,612 yards. Visitor policy and course guide.

southport-old-links.title: Southport Old Links Golf Club — Visitor Guide & Green Fees
southport-old-links.desc: Hidden value — a genuine links at the most accessible price on the coast. Green fee £40–£60. Par 71, 6,318 yards. Full visitor guide.

homePage.affiliateCtaTitle: Ready to Book Your Lake District Trip?
homePage.affiliateCtaDesc: Book accommodation in Keswick, Ambleside, Glenridding and across the Lake District. B&Bs, inns and hotels for walkers.
homePage.affiliateCtaDisclaimer: Booking.com affiliate link — we earn a commission at no extra cost to you.
homePage.editorialPara1: The Lake District has 214 Wainwright fells. People travel from Japan, Germany and the US specifically to complete them. The official DMO sites handle it poorly — no per-fell depth, no route guides, no practical info for walkers.
homePage.editorialPara2: Within a compact area you have Scafell Pike (England's highest), Helvellyn (Striding Edge), Skiddaw, Blencathra and 210 more. You can walk a different fell every day for months. The variety is striking — from family-friendly Catbells to the challenge of the Scafell massif.
homePage.editorialPara3: The practical case for the Lake District is simple: it's accessible from Manchester and Glasgow airports, the accommodation is walker-friendly, and the fells are free to access. No permits, no booking. Just a map, decent boots and a weather check.
homePage.editorialPara4: If you've ever thought about walking the Wainwrights, or just want a few days on the fells, this is the guide. Route info, heights, grid references, conditions and where to stay.
homePage.featureCard1Text: 90 min from Manchester Airport, 2 hours from Glasgow. Keswick, Ambleside and Windermere are the main bases.
homePage.featureCard2Text: All fells are open access. No permits, no booking. Just a map, boots and a weather check.
homePage.featureCard3Text: Accommodation with boot drying, early breakfast and drying rooms. B&Bs and inns built for walkers.
homePage.lakesGuideDesc: Accommodation, restaurants, villages and things to do in the Lake District.
homePage.lakesWildlifeDesc: Wildlife and nature reserves — ospreys, red deer, red squirrels and more.

open.pageDesc: Your guide to The 155th Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, Southport. History, tickets, accommodation and course guide.
open.stayTitle: Where to Stay for The Open

openPage.aboutTitle: The Open Championship at Royal Birkdale
openPage.aboutPara1: The Open Championship — the oldest Major in golf and the only one played outside North America — returns to Royal Birkdale for the 11th time in July 2026. No other venue on the Open rota has hosted more frequently, a record that reflects both the quality of the course and its exceptional logistical suitability for a major championship.
openPage.aboutPara2: Royal Birkdale is a links in the truest sense. Fairways run between towering sand dunes that funnel the coastal wind in unpredictable ways. The willow scrub rough — a Birkdale signature — is punishing but navigable. Greens are small, fast and subtly contoured. Championship conditions produce scoring that typically ends around level par or better for the winner, but only when the weather cooperates. When it doesn't, Birkdale becomes one of the most demanding tests in world golf.
openPage.aboutPara3: The 2017 Open delivered one of the most dramatic finishes in recent memory. Jordan Spieth, leading wire-to-wire, made a triple bogey on 13 after pulling his tee shot into an area containing TV equipment. His subsequent decision to take relief under the drop zone rule — combined with a remarkable pitch to within feet of the hole — became one of the defining images of modern Major championship golf.
openPage.aboutPara4: For golf tourists, The Open at Royal Birkdale represents a unique opportunity: the world's most prestigious golf event, at one of England's greatest links courses, in a compact and accessible setting. Spectator access throughout the course is excellent, the atmosphere is distinctly British, and the food and entertainment village has improved considerably in recent editions.
openPage.champNote2017: Wire-to-wire — memorable drop zone escape on 13
openPage.champNote1971: Third consecutive Major in six weeks
openPage.champNote1961: Palmer's legendary iron recovery from a bush on 15
openPage.ticketsAlertDesc: The R&A sells all tickets directly. There is no official third-party seller. Championship day tickets (Thursday–Sunday) sell out quickly — often within hours of release. Register for the R&A waiting list and ballot at TheOpen.com now.
openPage.ticketNote1: Usually most accessible tickets, good course access
openPage.ticketNote2: Book via TheOpen.com as soon as available
openPage.ticketNote3: Must be accompanied by a valid adult ticket holder
openPage.stayIntroDesc: Accommodation within 10 miles of Royal Birkdale books out 12–18 months before Open week. If you haven't booked yet, look at Liverpool or Formby as alternatives — both are manageable with early planning.
openPage.accomArea1Desc: Walking distance or short taxi to Royal Birkdale. The Bold Hotel on Lord Street is the traditional choice. Book 12+ months ahead for Open week.
openPage.accomArea2Desc: B&Bs and holiday lets in a quieter setting, 20 min drive to Birkdale. FormbyGuide.co.uk has local accommodation listings.
openPage.accomArea3Desc: Wide hotel choice, 45–60 min by car or train. Better availability but requires daily travel.
openPage.accomArea4Desc: Direct motorway access, 60–75 min. Useful if combining with other travel.
openPage.gettingThereItem2: Southport town centre ~2 miles — shuttle buses during Open week
openPage.gettingThereItem5: Merseyrail to Birkdale station: 8-min walk to course
openPage.onTheDayItem1: Gates open 2 hours before first tee time
openPage.onTheDayItem2: Dress code: smart casual, flat shoes recommended
openPage.onTheDayItem3: Mobile phones permitted (silent mode, no video)
openPage.spectatorTipsItem1: Practice days offer closest course access and photo opportunities
openPage.spectatorTipsItem2: Holes 17 and 18 pack out — position early for finish
openPage.spectatorTipsItem3: The 1st and 13th tees give good early views of field size
openPage.spectatorTipsItem4: Birkdale's natural dune grandstands require good shoes
openPage.spectatorTipsItem5: Check R&A app for real-time leaderboard and player tracking
openPage.playCourseItem1: Visitor tee times restart after championship setup is removed
openPage.playCourseItem2: Post-Open tee times book fast — Royal Birkdale handles direct
openPage.playCourseItem3: Combine with Hillside or S&A for a full Birkdale area trip
openPage.playCourseItem4: Caddies available — book well in advance for premium experience
openPage.bookAccomDesc: Don't leave it. Open week accommodation books out fast. Book via Booking.com for the widest choice and flexible cancellation.
openPage.playBirkdaleDesc: Planning a round before or after The Open? Green fees, visitor policy and booking guide.

teeTimesPage.pageTitle: How to Get a Tee Time
teeTimesPage.pageDesc: Visitor policies, booking processes and what to expect at each course — including Formby Golf Club's notoriously confusing process, explained clearly.
teeTimesPage.formbyCalloutTitle: Formby Golf Club — The Complicated One
teeTimesPage.formbyCalloutIntro: Formby Golf Club generates more visitor confusion than any other course on the Sefton Coast. Here's the full picture:
teeTimesPage.formbyPoint1Text: Formby Golf Club is a men-only private club. This is not unusual for traditional English golf clubs but is worth knowing before you arrive with a mixed group.
teeTimesPage.formbyPoint2Text: There is no tee time booking system on the website. You need to write or call the club secretary directly.
teeTimesPage.formbyPoint3Text: Access is primarily through clubs with formal reciprocal agreements. Ask your home club secretary if they have an arrangement with Formby.
teeTimesPage.formbyPoint4Text: A formal letter from your home club professional or secretary significantly increases chances of visitor access.
teeTimesPage.formbyPoint5Title: Formby Ladies Golf Club is separate.
teeTimesPage.formbyPoint5Text: Adjacent but entirely independent — its own course, members, visitor policy. More open to visitors than the men's club.
teeTimesPage.formbyPoint6Text: If you can get on, do. The course is exceptional — woodland pines and links dunes combined uniquely.
teeTimesPage.handicapRequirementText: Men's certificate required (limit {limit}). Bring your club handicap card or EGA card.
teeTimesPage.tip1Text: All courses on the Sefton Coast except Southport Old Links require a handicap certificate. Your national golf association card or EGA card is accepted.
teeTimesPage.tip2Text: The Sefton Coast is busy June–September. Royal Birkdale and Hillside fill 4–8 weeks ahead in peak season. Don't show up and hope.
teeTimesPage.tip3Text: Smart casual minimum at all clubs. No denim, no trainers, no t-shirts at most. Some clubs require collar and tailored trousers in the clubhouse.
teeTimesPage.tip4Text: Links courses often require a walk from car park to first tee. You want time to warm up and ask the pro shop about local rules and any temporary conditions.
teeTimesPage.tip5Title: Ask about the drop zone system
teeTimesPage.tip5Text: Royal Birkdale has specific drop zones and areas of special interest under local rules. Get the local rules card from the pro shop.
teeTimesPage.tip6Title: Weather changes fast on the coast
teeTimesPage.tip6Text: Pack a waterproof even if the morning looks clear. Coastal weather is unpredictable. A SW wind that's fresh in the morning can be strong by the back nine.

accommodationPage.pageDesc: Curated accommodation in the Lake District — selected for boot drying, early breakfast, and proximity to the fells.
accommodationPage.tip1: Book well ahead in summer. Keswick, Ambleside and Glenridding fill weeks ahead. Mid-week is easier than weekends.
accommodationPage.tip2: Ask about boot drying when booking. Good walkers accommodation will have a drying room or boot store.
accommodationPage.tip3: Base yourself according to your fells. Keswick for northern fells, Ambleside for central and southern, Glenridding for Helvellyn.
accommodationPage.tip4: Flexible cancellation matters. Mountain weather is unpredictable. Book refundable rates when possible.

scorecardPage.pageDesc: Par, yardage from all tees, course rating and slope rating for every Sefton Coast course. Use to plan your game and set expectations before you arrive.
scorecardPage.courseRatingDesc: The expected score for a scratch golfer (zero handicap) from that tee. A rating of 76.5 means a scratch player would be expected to shoot 76.5 in normal conditions.
scorecardPage.slopeRatingDesc: Measures difficulty relative to a bogey golfer. The USGA scale runs from 55 to 155 — a slope of 113 is average, 148 (Royal Birkdale championship) is extremely difficult.
scorecardPage.yardageMetresDesc: English clubs use yards. Metres are provided for continental European visitors. GPS devices and rangefinders are widely permitted — check with each club before use in competitions.
scorecardPage.dataAccuracyDesc: Data verified to best available sources. Course ratings and slope may be updated periodically by the club. Always confirm with the club before competition use.

itinerariesPage.pageDesc: Pre-built walking circuits for every duration and budget. Filter by days, budget and difficulty to find the right itinerary.
itinerariesPage.plannerDesc: Filter by days and budget to find walking circuits that match.
itinerariesPage.howToPlanTitle: How to Plan a Lake District Walking Trip
itinerariesPage.howToPlanPara1: The Lake District is compact enough that a two-day trip can cover two or three fells without difficult logistics. Keswick gives access to Skiddaw, Blencathra and Catbells. Ambleside and Langdale cover the southern and central fells. Glenridding and Patterdale are the base for Helvellyn and the eastern fells.
itinerariesPage.howToPlanPara2: For multi-day trips, base yourself in one area. Keswick has the widest accommodation choice and good transport. Ambleside is central. Glenridding and Buttermere are quieter but more limited. Book accommodation well ahead in summer.
itinerariesPage.howToPlanPara3: The order matters. Don't do Scafell Pike on day one if you're not hill-fit. Build up: start with Catbells or Latrigg, move to Blencathra or Helvellyn, save the big days for when your legs are in. Check the weather each morning.
itinerariesPage.howToPlanPara4: Book accommodation before you arrive. Popular bases fill weeks ahead in summer. Check MWIS and the Met Office mountain forecast each morning. Take a map and compass. Mobile coverage is patchy on the fells.

conditions.pageDesc: Weather and visibility for Lake District fells — Scafell Pike, Helvellyn, Skiddaw and more

conditionsPage.pageDesc: Weather and visibility for Lake District fells — updated manually each morning. Check MWIS and Met Office before setting out.
conditionsPage.aboutLinksPara1: Mountain weather changes fast. What looks clear from the valley can be clagged in on the summit. Wind strength increases with height. A 15 mph breeze at Grasmere can feel like 30 mph on Helvellyn.
conditionsPage.aboutLinksPara2: Check MWIS (Mountain Weather Information Service) and the Met Office mountain forecast before any walk. This page gives a quick overview for popular fells but is not a substitute for proper planning.
conditionsPage.aboutLinksPara3: Visibility matters as much as wind. Navigation on the fells requires a map and compass when the cloud is down. Clear conditions mean summit views and easier route-finding.
conditionsPage.conditionsDisclaimer: Conditions data is approximate and manually maintained. Always check MWIS and Met Office before travel. Hike The Lakes accepts no liability for conditions that differ from this page.

contactPage.featuredListingsText: Golf clubs, golf accommodation, caddie services and post-round dining can enquire about featured placement. Email with your business details.

contactPage.beforeContactItem3: → For mountain rescue, call 999 and ask for Police then Mountain Rescue.

legalPage.privacyAffiliateDesc: Some links on this site are affiliate links (Booking.com, OS Maps). These are clearly labelled. Clicking them may result in a commission to HikeTheLakes.com at no extra cost to you.

legalPage.infoAccuracyDesc: Route data, heights and conditions are provided in good faith and updated regularly. Always check MWIS and the Met Office before travel. HikeTheLakes accepts no liability for outdated information or changed conditions.

footer.tagline: The definitive guide to Lake District fells. Scafell Pike, Helvellyn, Skiddaw and more. Route guides and walking circuits.

royal-birkdale.tagline: Host of The Open Championship 2026 — the pinnacle of links golf
royal-birkdale.description:
Royal Birkdale is the greatest links course most golfers will ever play. Set among towering sand dunes on the Sefton Coast, it has hosted ten Open Championships and ranks consistently among the top five courses in the world. The 2026 Open returns here in July — and if you're planning a golf trip to the Sefton Coast, this is the reason.

The course demands creativity and intelligence. Fairways run between steep dunes that channel the wind in unpredictable ways. The rough is brutal. Greens are fast, subtle and positioned to make you think about every approach. It's not a course you muscle your way around — links experience helps enormously.

Visitor tee times are available, but Royal Birkdale is strict about both availability and etiquette. Pre-book well in advance, respect the dress code, and play to your handicap certificate requirement.
royal-birkdale.visitorPolicy: Visitors welcome subject to availability. Handicap certificate required (men 24, ladies 36). No visitors on competition days.

hillside.tagline: The finest links course you've never heard of — until now
hillside.description:
Hillside is the best-kept secret on the Sefton Coast — though not for much longer. Immediately adjacent to Royal Birkdale, it shares the same dramatic duneland but offers significantly better visitor access at a fraction of the green fee. Golf professionals who play both courses often rate Hillside's back nine among the finest in England.

The course underwent significant redevelopment on its final nine holes, which now run through spectacular ridge-and-valley terrain through the dunes. The routing is logical and the course condition is consistently excellent. If you're building a multi-day itinerary, Hillside the day before Royal Birkdale is a near-perfect combination.
hillside.visitorPolicy: Visitors welcome most weekdays and some weekends. Handicap certificate required.

formby.tagline: A private gem — one of England's finest inland links
formby.description:
Formby Golf Club is one of the most exclusive clubs on the Sefton Coast, and its visitor policy reflects that. The club has a reciprocal arrangement with a limited number of clubs and handles visitor requests on a case-by-case basis — you won't simply book a tee time online.

The course itself is exceptional. Set through coastal pinewoods with stretches of pure heathland links, it's a fundamentally different experience to Royal Birkdale or Hillside. The pines offer shelter and a distinctive visual character, while the dune sections are as challenging as anything on the coast. If you can get on, do.

Note: Formby Golf Club is a men-only club. Formby Ladies Golf Club (adjacent, founded 1896) is a separate and equally prestigious club with its own course and a more accessible visitor policy.
formby.visitorPolicy: Restricted visitor access. Reciprocal arrangements with approved clubs only. Contact the club secretary directly to enquire.

west-lancashire.tagline: One of England's oldest clubs — links golf at its purest
west-lancashire.description:
West Lancashire Golf Club, founded in 1873, is among the oldest golf clubs in England and one of the least visited on the Sefton Coast — primarily because it sits slightly further north at Blundellsands, outside the immediate Southport cluster.

This is a shame, because West Lancashire is a serious links test. The course has a raw, unmanicured quality that purists love — the rough is genuine rough, the greens are fast and true, and the wind off the Irish Sea is ever-present. It lacks the manicured polish of Royal Birkdale but delivers a more authentic, elemental links experience at a considerably more accessible green fee.

The club has a long history with Open Championship qualifying. If you want to play a genuinely historic and challenging links without the premium price tag, West Lancashire is a strong choice.
west-lancashire.visitorPolicy: Visitors welcome most weekdays. Weekends limited — phone ahead.

southport-ainsdale.tagline: Ryder Cup heritage and genuine links character at an accessible price
southport-ainsdale.description:
Southport & Ainsdale — known locally as S&A — hosted the Ryder Cup in 1933 and 1937, making it one of a tiny number of non-Royal venues with that distinction. The history alone makes it worth a round, but the course earns its reputation on its own merits too.

S&A sits between Hillside and Formby and shares the coastal dune terrain that characterises the best of the Sefton Coast. The first few holes are relatively benign, which gives visitors a false sense of security — the course stiffens considerably through the middle section, with several holes genuinely exposed to the prevailing westerly.

Green fees are among the most accessible on the coast for what is genuinely a historic and challenging links. For visitors putting together a multi-day trip, S&A pairs well with either Hillside or West Lancashire for a strong two-day itinerary at reasonable cost.
southport-ainsdale.visitorPolicy: Visitors welcome most weekdays. Weekends by arrangement. Open to societies.

southport-old-links.tagline: Hidden value — a genuine links at the most accessible price on the coast
southport-old-links.description:
Southport Old Links is the quiet one. Tucked away in Churchtown on the northern edge of Southport, it's the kind of club that gets overlooked on bucket-list itineraries — which is exactly why it's worth playing.

Founded in 1901 and set on genuine links terrain, Old Links offers a round that punches above its green fee significantly. The course is shorter than its Sefton Coast neighbours but plays longer than the card in a coastal breeze, and the bunkering is thoughtfully placed to reward proper links shot-making.

For visitors building a high-volume trip — four or five rounds — Old Links provides welcome relief on the wallet while still delivering authentic links golf. It's also the most welcoming to visitors without handicap certificates, making it the natural choice for higher-handicappers or casual players.
southport-old-links.visitorPolicy: Very welcoming to visitors most days of the week. Most accessible club on the coast.
